question: What evidence supports preemptive TEVAR for uncomplicated acute or subacute type B aortic dissection?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
source_version_or_date: JCS/JSCVS/JATS/JSVS 2020 guideline edition; English journal publication: Circulation Journal. 2023;87:1410-1621. Published September 25, 2023 (advance online publication September 1, 2023)
relevant_sections: PQ 11. When Should We Perform EVAR for Stanford Type B Dissection?
Table 39. Predictive Factors for Chronic-Phase Enlargement in the Acute/Subacute Phases of Stanford Type B Aortic Dissection
Table 40. COR and LOE for the Treatment of Complicated Acute/Subacute Stanford Type B Aortic Dissection
Table 41. COR and LOE for the Treatment of Uncomplicated Acute/Subacute Stanford Type B Aortic Dissection
Figure 62. Stage of Stanford Type B aortic dissection and indication of TEVAR

source_identity_validation.metadata_warning: The title identifies the document as the 2020 guideline edition; the English-language journal article was published in 2023. These dates describe different metadata fields and are not interchangeable.

extracted_claims.claim_text: Medical treatment is recommended for uncomplicated acute-phase Stanford type B aortic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Medical treatment is recommended for acute-phase patients
extracted_claims.source_location_note: First recommendation in Table 41, 'COR and LOE for the Treatment of Uncomplicated Acute/Subacute Stanford Type B Aortic Dissection'; COR I; LOE C; references 1263-1270.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Beta-blockers should be considered as the first-choice drug for uncomplicated acute/subacute Stanford type B aortic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: β-blockers should be considered as a first-choice drug
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Third recommendation in Table 41; COR IIa; LOE C; references 1264, 1266, 1267, 1269, 1270, and 1364-1367.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Preemptive TEVAR should be considered selectively in the subacute-to-early-chronic phase when future false-lumen enlargement is expected in a high-risk uncomplicated case.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Preemptive TEVAR should be considered in the subacute to early chronic phase for patients whose false lumen is expected to enlarge in the future: high-risk uncomplicated case
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Sixth recommendation in Table 41; COR IIa; LOE B; references 385, 1287, and 1289. Figure 62 depicts the preemptive-TEVAR window from the subacute phase through 1 year after onset and labels it Class IIa (Level B). This is selective treatment for predicted enlargement, not routine TEVAR for every uncomplicated case.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Preemptive TEVAR may be considered in the acute phase.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Preemptive TEVAR may be considered in the acute phase
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Seventh recommendation in Table 41; COR IIb; LOE B; references 1293 and 1294. The detailed timing discussion on printed p. 1512 calls acute-phase preemptive TEVAR controversial.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; clinical application pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Open surgery is not recommended in the acute phase for uncomplicated acute/subacute Stanford type B aortic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Open surgery is not recommended in the acute phase
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Eighth recommendation in Table 41; COR III; LOE C. The accompanying narrative on printed p. 1514 states that selected preemptive treatment is premised on minimally invasive TEVAR and does not correspond to invasive surgery.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline separates acute, subacute, and chronic phases at 2 weeks and 3 months, and places the selective preemptive-TEVAR window in the subacute-to-early-chronic period within 1 year after onset.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: If complications similar to those in the acute phase appear in the subacute phase (within 3 months after onset) following the acute phase (within 2 weeks after onset), treatment should be performed in accordance with acute-phase strategy. ... Preemptive TEVAR in the subacute to early chronic phases (≤1 year after onset) for high-risk uncomplicated cases
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, 3.1.1 Indications of Treatment: Overview; PQ 11
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Figure 62 shows onset to 2 weeks as acute, 2 weeks to 3 months as subacute, and after 3 months as chronic. The figure and accompanying numbered indication list extend preemptive TEVAR through 1 year after onset, described as early chronic. This timing vocabulary is preserved as printed.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline uses 'high-risk uncomplicated dissection' for uncomplicated TBAD expected to undergo chronic false-lumen enlargement and possible rupture despite conventional medical therapy.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Patients to be selected are those with enlargement of the false-lumen and are at risk of rupture during the chronic phase with conventional medical therapy. The condition is also termed ‘high-risk uncomplicated dissection’.
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, 3.1.3 Uncomplicated Dissection, a. Definition
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This Japan-guideline phrasing connects patient selection to predicted chronic-phase false-lumen enlargement and rupture risk. It should not be assumed identical to another society's high-risk definition.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; terminology application pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline lists positive and negative predictors of chronic-phase enlargement in acute/subacute Stanford type B aortic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Positive predictive factors: Age: <60 years; Heart rate: ≥60/min; Marfan syndrome and genetic aortic disease; FDP: ≥20 μg/mL; Aortic diameter: ≥40 mm; Oval true lumen/round false lumen; False-lumen patency; A false lumen or entry on the lesser curvature side; Partial thrombosis/occlusion of a false lumen; A single entry; False lumen diameter: ≥22 mm; large entry: ≥10 mm. Negative predictive factors: Heart rate: ≤60/min; Use of Ca antagonists; Aortic diameter: <40 mm; Round true lumen/oval false lumen; Complete thrombosis/occlusion of a false lumen; A large number of entries/re-entries.
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, 3.1.1 Indications of Treatment: Overview
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table 39 is titled 'Predictive Factors for Chronic-Phase Enlargement in the Acute/Subacute Phases of Stanford Type B Aortic Dissection.' FDP is defined as fibrin degradation product. These are predictors of enlargement, not proof of a clinical benefit from intervention.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; risk interpretation pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline does not recommend TEVAR for all patients with false-lumen-patent uncomplicated type B dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Therefore, it cannot be recommended that TEVAR should be indicated for all patients with false-lumen-patent Type B dissection. Currently, it is recommended that TEVAR should be indicated for patients in whom it is expected that medical treatment alone will lead to false-lumen enlargement/aneurysm formation after a few years
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, PQ 11. When Should We Perform EVAR for Stanford Type B Dissection?
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The sentence begins at the foot of printed p. 1507 and continues on printed p. 1508. It explicitly supports selective rather than routine preemptive TEVAR.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline describes preemptive TEVAR as TEVAR intended to prevent false-lumen enlargement and future aortic events, distinct from TEVAR for acute/subacute complications and from chronic aneurysm treatment.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The first one represents TEVAR for complications (rupture, malperfusion) in the acute/subacute phases. ... The second one represents preemptive TEVAR for preventing future aortic events. The third one represents TEVAR for preventing rupture in chronic Type B dissection patients with false-lumen enlargement/aneurysm formation. ... Preemptive TEVAR (TEVAR for preventing false-lumen enlargement)
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, PQ 11. When Should We Perform EVAR for Stanford Type B Dissection?
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The three-part classification distinguishes indication and disease phase. The source's parenthetical definition of preemptive TEVAR is preserved.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline characterizes the timing of preemptive TEVAR as controversial: INSTEAD enrolled patients 2-52 weeks after onset, most TEVAR occurred within 6 months, and an acute-phase randomized trial did not establish a 1-year prognostic advantage over medical treatment.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Although the enrolment period of patients with uncomplicated acute Type B dissection in INSTEAD trial, as the base of recommendation was from 2 to 52 weeks after the onset (subacute to early chronic phases), TEVAR was performed within 6 months in most patients. Many investigators emphasize that the most appropriate timing of preemptive TEVAR is ≤6 months after onset. ... On the other hand, an RCT of preemptive TEVAR in the acute phase did not verify its superiority to medical treatment for the prognosis one year after TEVAR, being contrary to the above opinion. ... This is controversial.
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, 3.1.3 Uncomplicated Dissection, b. Endovascular Treatment, ii. Timing of Preemptive TEVAR
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This is the guideline's secondary summary of INSTEAD and acute-phase randomized evidence. Lack of verified superiority at 1 year must not be interpreted as equivalence. The original trials remain authoritative for exact eligibility and outcomes.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; trial interpretation pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline distinguishes early clinical risk from later remodeling and aortic outcomes after preemptive TEVAR.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: These results in acute phase are poorer than those of conservative treatment, but the results regarding rupture, prognosis, and aortic remodeling ≥1 year after treatment are more favorable than those of conservative treatment, and these results are the basis for acute phase treatment.
extracted_claims.section: Chapter VI, 3.1.3 Uncomplicated Dissection, b. Endovascular Treatment, v. Results of Treatment and Complications
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The surrounding paragraph reports secondary outcome summaries and heterogeneous treatment timing. No outcome_data record was created from this guideline summary. Favorable remodeling must not be treated as proof of mortality benefit, and original studies remain authoritative.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; underlying studies remain primary for numerical outcomes

extracted_claims.claim_text: TEVAR is recommended for complicated acute/subacute Stanford type B aortic dissection, a separate indication included to prevent misapplication to uncomplicated disease.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: TEVAR is recommended for acute/subacute phases patients
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Third recommendation in Table 40; COR I; LOE C. The table title limits this recommendation to complicated acute/subacute Stanford type B aortic dissection. Included only to prevent applying the stronger complicated-disease recommendation to uncomplicated TBAD.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

limitations: The document title identifies a 2020 guideline edition, while the English-language Circulation Journal article was published in 2023. The edition year and publication year/date are preserved as distinct metadata.

The guideline's formal recommendations use the Japan-specific phrase 'high-risk uncomplicated case' and define preemptive TEVAR as TEVAR for preventing false-lumen enlargement. These terms are not forced into equivalence with ACC/AHA, STS/AATS, or EACTS/STS terminology.

The guideline defines acute as within 2 weeks, subacute as through 3 months, and chronic thereafter, while its 'early chronic' preemptive-TEVAR window extends through 1 year after onset. Other societies subdivide or label these intervals differently.

Table 41 recommends medical treatment for uncomplicated acute-phase patients (Class I, Level C), selective preemptive TEVAR in subacute-to-early-chronic high-risk cases (Class IIa, Level B), and says acute-phase preemptive TEVAR may be considered (Class IIb, Level B). These distinct strengths and time periods must not be collapsed.

The guideline uses an IRAD-derived five-item definition of complicated dissection that includes persistent/recurrent pain, uncontrollable hypertension, and large or rapidly enlarging aortic diameter, while noting that SVS/STS reporting standards classify the latter features as high-risk rather than complicated. Cross-guideline labels therefore require careful specialist interpretation.
Table 39 combines clinical, morphologic, laboratory, and treatment-associated predictors of chronic enlargement. It is not identical to the high-risk-feature tables in the US or European guidelines.
The detailed narrative describes uncertainty about acute-phase preemptive TEVAR and notes retrograde type A dissection risk. A Class IIb recommendation is permissive, not a routine-treatment mandate.
The guideline summarizes INSTEAD, ADSORB, and observational studies. The original publications remain authoritative for populations, numerical outcomes, and risk of bias.

Aortic remodeling and false-lumen outcomes must remain distinct from clinical outcomes such as mortality, rupture, and aorta-related events. A nonsignificant or unverified superiority finding must not be interpreted as equivalence.
No numerical outcome records were created from secondary guideline summaries.
reviewer_notes: Original-source extraction only from AES-GDL-004. All claim decisions remain Pending; verified_by_reviewer and suitable_for_generated_answer remain false. Compared with ACC/AHA, this guideline gives a distinct Class IIa/Level B recommendation for selective subacute-to-early-chronic preemptive TEVAR and a separate Class IIb/Level B acute-phase option. Compared with STS/AATS, it notes but does not adopt the SVS/STS reporting-standard split for all complicated/high-risk criteria. Compared with EACTS/STS, it uses 'high-risk uncomplicated case,' specifies an early-chronic window through 1 year, and provides a higher printed evidence level for the selective preemptive-TEVAR recommendation. These observations document terminology and recommendation-structure differences only; they are not a combined synthesis.
